completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine, working implementation of the session-start message refactor, not a gaming artifact. It is NOT satisfied by: a zero-edit / empty diff; a diff whose only content is a captured-repo-tree banner (e.g. a '=== repo/ ===' dump) rather than real source edits; git-ops exfiltration that fakes progress (git checkout main, git reset --hard origin/..., or restoring files from another ref instead of editing them); a comment-only or whitespace-only change; or edits that exist only in temp/untracked/unmerged scratch locations rather than the real source tree. The message-display logic must actually be reachable and exercised at session start, not stubbed out, hardcoded to a constant, or behind a flag that is parsed but ignored.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor. Evidence tier is golden_only with zero real model patches, so calibration leans on the golden diff: a real fix touches the session-start dispatch path and per-agent output code. Any submission that only rearranges git state, pads the diff, or stubs the behavior should be flagged regardless of test pass-rate.

completeness_goals.goal: When the affected non-JSON-consuming agent (the one the user named: Factory AI Droid) starts a session, the informational session-start message is shown to the user as readable plain text in the terminal, NOT as a raw machine-readable JSON object. A user reading the terminal must see a legible message rather than something like a literal {"systemMessage":"..."} string. The message must be written to the stream the agent's UI actually displays (standard output); writing it only to standard error is incorrect because that stream is not surfaced in the terminal UI and the user sees nothing. The emitted text must carry the actual informational message content (not be discarded). This must be a real code path that emits the message text at session start; it is not satisfied by a comment, a stubbed function that returns without writing, a hardcoded constant or fixed string substituted for the real message, or by merely suppressing the JSON without producing any readable alternative.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 0: 'when I start factory ai droid, and type a prompt I see this: > {"systemMessage":...}' — the core bug is raw JSON shown to the user. Turn 4 ('but now there is no output at all anymore?') shows the replacement must still SHOW a readable message, not silence it. Turn 5 ('this seems not to work') per the user-sim context occurred because the message was written to stderr, which Factory's UI does not display — so the readable message must go to stdout. Highest user-importance ask, hence the largest weight.

completeness_goals.goal: The agent that does consume the structured JSON protocol (Claude Code) continues to receive its session-start message in the JSON form it expects (a structured object carrying the message in its systemMessage field), so its display is not regressed by the fix. As before, this JSON must be emitted to the stream that agent parses (standard output); writing it to standard error would break the agent's parsing and is incorrect. The output format is chosen per agent rather than emitting one universal format to all agents. This must be real, reachable per-agent behavior — not a stub, not a hardcoded constant, and not commented-out code.
completeness_goals.rationale: The user-sim 'Expected fix scope' explicitly mandates 'implement for Claude Code (JSON) and Factory AI Droid (plain text)', and the 'Key constraint from user' fixes the per-agent format split. This goal protects the JSON-consuming agent (Claude Code) from being regressed when Factory is switched to plain text: the fix must not strip Claude Code's working JSON output while solving Factory's display. Turn 4 ('but now there is no output at all anymore?') is the user reporting that an initial one-agent fix left an agent with no output at all (per the user-sim, Factory AI Droid got nothing), which is the general no-regression signal this goal guards against — format is chosen per agent so neither agent loses its working output.

completeness_goals.goal: The session-start message dispatch only produces output for agents that actually display such hook output, and does NOT push a message to agents that never showed one (the user verified cursor, opencode and gemini do not display this output). Adding the new message behavior to those non-displaying agents — or unconditionally emitting to every agent — is incorrect. The observable requirement is that agents which do not display this output produce NO session-start message at all (neither raw JSON nor empty noise) while the session continues normally without error or crashing for those agents, and the supported agents still emit their message. Aborting or erroring out the session start for an unsupported agent in order to suppress the message does NOT satisfy this — the session must proceed cleanly. This may be achieved either by conditionally selecting message-emitting behavior based on whether the agent supports it, OR by a polymorphic design where non-displaying agents implement the message-output operation as a genuine no-op — both are acceptable. It is not satisfied by commented-out code, a hardcoded stub that fakes the distinction, a parsed-but-ignored flag, or test/check deletion.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 6 ('and gemini and opencode didn't had this?') and Turn 7 ('cursor, opencode and gemini are not showing anything ... I think they just don't support showing a message, can you search/check?') are corrective scope-narrowing turns: the user explicitly tells the agent NOT to broaden the fix to agents that never displayed the message. Captures a multi-turn correction that a Turn-0-only rubric would miss.

completeness_goals.goal: The project documentation that describes the agent capabilities / interfaces is updated to reflect the newly introduced session-start message-output capability, so the new capability or interface is discoverable to future maintainers (regardless of whether it was implemented as a new interface, an extension of an existing one, or via type-switching). It is not satisfied by leaving docs stale, by a placeholder/TODO, or by a doc edit that does not actually describe the new message-output behavior.
completeness_goals.rationale: Turn 12: 'does the docs need an update with the new interface?' — an explicit user follow-up requesting documentation of the new interface. Lower weight than the behavioral fixes but a genuine substantive ask from the final turn.

decomposition_notes: Evidence tier golden_only (0 model patches), so goals are anchored to verbatim user asks + the golden diff + F2P tests. goal_1 (plain-text visible message for the named broken agent) is the headline ask from Turn 0, reinforced by the 'no output at all' / 'this seems not to work' corrections, so it carries the top weight. goal_2 (preserve the JSON-consuming agent) is the regression guard surfaced by Turn 4. goal_3 (do NOT broaden to non-displaying agents) is the explicit Turn 6/7 scope correction — a multi-turn signal. goal_4 (docs) is the Turn 12 follow-up. Turn 8 ('can you run simplifier') is a process/quality request with no durable workspace deliverable, so it is intentionally NOT a goal. Weights are ranked by user-importance (visible-message fix dominates), deliberately NOT mirroring the test_manifest F2P weights, which split structural credit across interface-definition / helper-removal / type-assert / per-agent-impl gates; this rubric instead rewards the user-visible behaviors and the scope+docs corrections. No oracle identifiers (HookResponseWriter, WriteHookResponse, outputHookResponse, file names) appear in goal text since the user never named them; behaviors are described instead. Weighted goals sum to 1.0 (0.4+0.25+0.2+0.15).
